Acanthis flammea: Redpolls can survive temperatures as low as -54°C by storing seeds in a special throat pouch to digest overnight for warmth.
Accipiter nisus: Female sparrowhawks are up to 25% larger than males. One of the biggest size differences between sexes of any bird.
Acrocephalus schoenobaenus: Sedge warblers fatten up so much before migrating to Africa that they can fly 4,000 km non-stop without feeding.
Acrocephalus scirpaceus: Reed warblers are the main host of the cuckoo. They've evolved to recognise and reject fake eggs, sparking an evolutionary arms race.
Actitis hypoleucos: Common sandpipers bob their tails constantly while walking. A habit so distinctive it earned them the old folk name 'willy wagtail'.
Aegithalos caudatus: Long-tailed tits build incredibly elastic nests from spider silk and lichen, stretching to accommodate a brood of up to 12 chicks.
Alauda arvensis: Skylarks sing while hovering up to 300 metres high. Their song flights can last over an hour without a break.
Alcedo atthis: Kingfishers hit the water at up to 25 mph and have a third eyelid that acts like swimming goggles to protect their eyes underwater.
Alectoris rufa: Red-legged partridges were introduced to Britain in the 1770s as a gamebird. They're actually native to southwestern Europe.
Anas acuta: Northern pintails are one of the fastest ducks in flight, reaching speeds of over 65 mph during migration.
Anas crecca: The Eurasian teal is the smallest dabbling duck in Europe. An adult weighs about the same as a can of beans.
Anas platyrhynchos: Mallards sleep with one eye open. Literally. Half their brain stays awake to watch for predators.
Anser albifrons: White-fronted geese can live for over 20 years and return to the exact same wintering fields year after year.
Anser anser: Greylag geese are the ancestors of most domestic geese. They were first domesticated at least 4,000 years ago in Egypt.
Anser brachyrhynchus: Almost the entire world population of pink-footed geese, around 500,000 birds, winters in the UK.
Anthus pratensis: Meadow pipits are the most common host of cuckoos in upland Britain. A single cuckoo may parasitise dozens of pipit nests.
Apus apus: Swifts can stay airborne for up to 10 months straight, eating, sleeping, and even mating on the wing without ever landing.
Ardea alba: Great egrets were nearly hunted to extinction in the 1800s. Their feathers were worth more than gold by weight for hat decorations.
Ardea cinerea: Grey herons can strike at prey in just 0.02 seconds. Faster than most animals can react to dodge.
Arenaria interpres: Turnstones got their name from their habit of flipping over stones, seaweed, and even dead fish to find food hiding underneath.
Asio flammeus: Short-eared owls are one of the few owls that hunt in broad daylight, quartering low over grassland like a harrier.
Aythya ferina: Common pochards can dive to over 2 metres deep and hold their breath for up to 30 seconds while feeding underwater.
Aythya fuligula: Tufted ducks only colonised Britain in the 1800s, spreading rapidly after zebra mussels, their favourite food, invaded British waterways.
Botaurus stellaris: Bitterns produce one of the loudest bird calls in Europe. Their booming can be heard up to 5 km away on a still night.
Branta canadensis: Canada geese were introduced to Britain in the 1600s as ornamental birds for King Charles II's parks. They never left.
Branta leucopsis: Barnacle geese got their name from a medieval myth that they hatched from goose barnacles, since nobody had seen their Arctic nesting grounds.
Bucephala clangula: Goldeneyes nest in tree holes. Ducklings jump from heights of up to 15 metres on their first day of life, bouncing when they land.
Buteo buteo: Buzzards are now the UK's most common bird of prey, having recovered from near-extinction caused by pesticides and persecution in the 1960s.
Calidris alba: Sanderlings are the small waders you see sprinting along the tideline. They run so fast their legs blur, earning them the nickname 'clockwork birds'.
Calidris alpina: Dunlins can change the size of their digestive organs between seasons. Their gizzard grows larger in winter to process tougher food.
Calidris canutus: Red knots migrate up to 15,000 km from the Arctic to Africa. One individual, nicknamed 'Moonbird', flew enough miles to reach the Moon.
Calidris pugnax: Male ruffs have three completely different mating strategies: territorial fighters, sneaky satellites, and female mimics, all genetically determined.
Capreolus capreolus: Roe deer can delay pregnancy. After mating in summer, the embryo pauses development for months and only starts growing again in January.
Carduelis carduelis: Goldfinches are one of the few birds that can extract seeds from teasel heads, using their fine beaks like tweezers.
Certhia familiaris: Treecreepers always spiral up tree trunks, never down. They use their stiff tail feathers as a prop, like a tiny woodpecker.
Cettia cetti: Cetti's warbler only colonised Britain in the 1960s, spreading north as winters became milder. It's now resident along southern waterways.
Charadrius dubius: Little ringed plovers were first recorded breeding in Britain in 1938 at a gravel pit. They now rely almost entirely on man-made habitats.
Charadrius hiaticula: Ringed plovers fake a broken wing to lure predators away from their nest, dragging themselves along the ground in a convincing display.
Chlidonias niger: Black terns build floating nests from dead water plants. If the water level rises, they simply add more material to keep the eggs dry.
Chloris chloris: Greenfinches can eat seeds that would be toxic to most birds. Their liver is especially efficient at breaking down plant toxins.
Chroicocephalus ridibundus: Despite the name, black-headed gulls actually have chocolate-brown heads. And only in summer. In winter their heads turn white.
Circus aeruginosus: Marsh harriers pass food to each other mid-air. The female flips upside down to catch prey dropped by the male in a spectacular aerial transfer.
Coloeus monedula: Jackdaws can follow human eye gaze to find hidden food. One of very few animals outside of apes that can do this.
Columba livia: All domestic pigeons descend from rock doves. Feral city pigeons are literally the same species returning to a wild lifestyle.
Columba oenas: Stock doves nest in rabbit burrows and tree holes rather than building their own nests. They're the only British pigeon that does this regularly.
Columba palumbus: Wood pigeons can drink by sucking water up through their beak like a straw. Most birds have to tilt their head back to swallow each sip.
Corvus corax: Ravens can solve multi-step puzzles, plan for the future, and have been observed sliding down snowy roofs repeatedly. Apparently just for fun.
Corvus cornix: Hooded crows in coastal areas have learned to drop shellfish from height onto rocks to crack them open. And they choose the right height to avoid wasting effort.
Corvus corone: Carrion crows have been seen placing walnuts on roads for cars to crack, then waiting for the traffic lights to change before collecting them.
Corvus frugilegus: Rooks can use tools to solve problems and have even been filmed bending wire into hooks. Skills once thought unique to great apes.
Cyanistes caeruleus: Blue tits learned to open milk bottle tops across Britain in the 1920s. The behaviour spread so fast it became a textbook example of cultural learning in animals.
Cygnus cygnus: Whooper swans migrate from Iceland to the UK. A 1,400 km journey over open ocean, often completed in a single non-stop flight.
Cygnus olor: Mute swans aren't actually mute. They hiss, grunt, and snort. The name just means they're quieter than whooper and Bewick's swans.
Delichon urbicum: House martins build their mud nests one beakful at a time. A single nest requires over 1,000 trips and takes about two weeks.
Dendrocopos major: Great spotted woodpeckers drum on trees at 20 beats per second. A spongy skull bone absorbs the shock and prevents brain damage.
Egretta garzetta: Little egrets were virtually unknown in Britain before 1989. They colonised naturally from France and are now a common sight on estuaries.
Emberiza citrinella: The yellowhammer's song sounds like 'a little bit of bread and no cheese'. It's one of the easiest British birdsongs to learn.
Emberiza schoeniclus: Male reed buntings sometimes sneak onto neighbouring territories to mate. DNA studies show up to 40% of chicks aren't fathered by the nest male.
Erinaceus europaeus: Hedgehogs are immune to adder venom and will happily eat venomous snakes. They're one of the few British mammals with natural venom resistance.
Erithacus rubecula: European robins are fiercely territorial. They've been recorded fighting to the death over a patch of garden. Their red breast is a threat signal, not a decoration.
Falco peregrinus: Peregrine falcons are the fastest animals on Earth, reaching over 240 mph in a hunting dive. Faster than a Formula 1 car at top speed.
Falco tinnunculus: Kestrels can see ultraviolet light, which lets them spot vole urine trails glowing on the ground. They literally follow a UV map to their prey.
Fringilla coelebs: The chaffinch got its Latin name 'coelebs' (meaning bachelor) because in Scandinavia, females migrate south for winter but males often stay behind.
Fringilla montifringilla: Bramblings form enormous winter roosts. The largest ever recorded in Europe held an estimated 70 million birds in a single Swiss forest.
Fulica atra: Coots are notoriously aggressive and will fight anything near their nest. They've been filmed attacking swans, dogs, and even model boats.
Gallinago gallinago: Snipe produce a strange 'drumming' sound by diving through the air. The noise comes from their outer tail feathers vibrating in the wind, not their voice.
Gallinula chloropus: Moorhen chicks from earlier broods help their parents raise the next batch of siblings. They're one of few British birds with teenage babysitters.
Garrulus glandarius: Jays bury up to 5,000 acorns each autumn and can remember where most of them are months later. Forgotten ones grow into oak trees.
Haematopus ostralegus: Despite the name 'oystercatcher', they rarely eat oysters. Their chisel-like bill is specialised for prising open mussels and cockles instead.
Hirundo rustica: Barn swallows migrate up to 11,000 km to southern Africa and back every year. An individual may travel 2 million km in its lifetime.
Larus argentatus: Herring gulls can drink both fresh and salt water. They have special glands above their eyes that filter out the salt.
Larus canus: Common gulls aren't actually the most common gull in Britain. Herring gulls and black-headed gulls both outnumber them significantly.
Larus fuscus: Lesser black-backed gulls migrate to West Africa for winter. They're one of the few British gull species that makes a true long-distance migration.
Larus marinus: Great black-backed gulls are the world's largest gull species and are powerful enough to swallow a whole puffin or rabbit.
Lepus europaeus: Brown hares can run at 45 mph, faster than any other British land mammal, and they don't burrow, sleeping in shallow scrapes called 'forms'.
Limosa lapponica: Bar-tailed godwits hold the record for the longest non-stop flight of any bird. One individual flew 13,560 km from Alaska to New Zealand without resting.
Limosa limosa: Black-tailed godwits were hunted to extinction in Britain by the 1830s. They naturally recolonised in the 1950s and are now carefully protected.
Linaria cannabina: Linnets were once the most popular cagebird in Britain, prized for their musical song. The practice was only banned in 1981.
Mareca penelope: Wigeon are the only British duck that regularly graze on grass like geese. Large flocks can mow estuary marshes flat over winter.
Mareca strepera: Gadwall are kleptoparasites. They steal food from diving birds like coots by snatching vegetation from their beaks when they surface.
Melanitta nigra: Common scoters spend almost their entire lives at sea and are one of the hardest British ducks to see on land.
Mergus merganser: Goosanders have serrated bill edges, like tiny teeth, that grip slippery fish, earning them the old nickname 'sawbill'.
Mergus serrator: Red-breasted mergansers have been clocked at 100 mph in level flight, making them one of the fastest flying birds ever recorded.
Micromys minutus: Harvest mice are Britain's smallest rodent. An adult weighs about the same as a 2p coin and builds tennis-ball-sized nests woven into tall grass.
Microtus agrestis: Field voles are the most numerous British mammal. There are an estimated 75 million of them, and they're the main food source for barn owls and kestrels.
Motacilla alba: Pied wagtails form enormous winter roosts in warm city centres. One famous roost on Dublin's O'Connell Street held over 3,600 birds in a single tree.
Motacilla cinerea: Despite the name, grey wagtails have bright yellow underparts. They're actually more colourful than the bird called 'yellow wagtail'.
Muscicapa striata: Spotted flycatchers catch insects by launching from a perch, snatching prey mid-air, and returning to the exact same spot. A hunting style called 'sallying'.
Mustela erminea: Stoats turn completely white in winter in northern regions (called ermine). Except for the black tip of their tail, which confuses predators.
Numenius arquata: Curlews have the longest bill of any European wader, up to 15 cm, perfectly curved to probe deep into mud for buried worms.
Numenius phaeopus: Whimbrels navigate using Earth's magnetic field during their 10,000 km migration. They can even detect the angle of magnetic field lines.
Nyctalus noctula: Noctule bats are the UK's largest bat species and can fly at 50 km/h. They're sometimes mistaken for swifts when hunting at dusk.
Oenanthe oenanthe: Northern wheatears make one of the longest migrations of any songbird. Greenland birds fly 15,000 km to West Africa, crossing open ocean.
Oryctolagus cuniculus: Rabbits were introduced to Britain by the Normans in the 12th century as a luxury food. They escaped from warrens and colonised the entire country.
Parus major: Great tits have been recorded hunting and eating bats in Hungarian caves. They learned to listen for bat echolocation calls to find them in the dark.
Passer domesticus: House sparrows take dust baths to control parasites. They wriggle in dry soil, working fine particles between their feathers to suffocate lice and mites.
Perdix perdix: Grey partridges hold the record for the largest clutch of any British bird. They can lay up to 22 eggs in a single nest.
Periparus ater: Coal tits hide thousands of seeds in bark crevices each autumn. They can remember individual hiding spots for up to 28 days.
Phalacrocorax carbo: Cormorants have to dry their wings after diving because, unlike most waterbirds, their feathers aren't fully waterproof. That's why you see them standing with wings spread.
Phasianus colchicus: Pheasants were brought to Britain by the Romans nearly 2,000 years ago. They're one of the country's longest-established introduced species.
Phoenicurus phoenicurus: Redstarts constantly quiver their fiery orange-red tails. The name literally means 'red tail' from the Old English 'steort'.
Phylloscopus collybita: Chiffchaffs are named after their song. A simple two-note 'chiff-chaff' repeated endlessly, making them one of the easiest warblers to identify by ear.
Phylloscopus sibilatrix: Wood warblers sing while performing a shivering display flight through the canopy. Their trilling song accelerates like a coin spinning on a table.
Phylloscopus trochilus: Willow warblers weigh just 10 grams but migrate 12,000 km to sub-Saharan Africa. One of the longest journeys relative to body size of any bird.
Pica pica: Magpies are one of the few animals that can recognise themselves in a mirror. A sign of self-awareness once thought unique to humans and great apes.
Platalea leucorodia: Spoonbills feed by sweeping their flattened bill from side to side through shallow water. Nerve endings in the bill snap it shut when they touch prey.
Pluvialis apricaria: Golden plovers in the UK were at the centre of a famous bet that led to the creation of the Guinness Book of Records in 1955.
Pluvialis squatarola: Grey plovers have a unique 'armpit' patch of black feathers visible in flight. It's the easiest way to tell them apart from golden plovers.
Podiceps cristatus: Great crested grebes perform one of the most elaborate courtship dances of any bird. Pairs mirror each other's movements while carrying waterweeds as gifts.
Poecile montanus: Willow tits excavate their own nest holes in rotten wood. They're one of the very few British songbirds that can do this.
Prunella modularis: Dunnocks have one of the most complex mating systems of any bird. A single female may mate with multiple males who all help raise the chicks.
Pyrrhula pyrrhula: Bullfinches were once considered such serious orchard pests that Tudor England put a bounty of one penny on their heads.
Rallus aquaticus: Water rails are heard far more often than seen. Their pig-like squealing calls from deep in reedbeds earned them the nickname 'sharming'.
Rattus norvegicus: Despite the name, brown rats didn't come from Norway. They originated in northern China and reached Britain on ships around 1728.
Recurvirostra avosetta: The avocet is the symbol of the RSPB. It was extinct in Britain for over 100 years before dramatically recolonising Suffolk in 1947.
Regulus regulus: Goldcrests are Britain's smallest bird at just 5 grams. Yet some migrate across the North Sea, an extraordinary feat for something that weighs less than a 20p coin.
Riparia riparia: Sand martins dig burrow nests up to a metre deep into riverbanks and sand cliffs. A pair can excavate the entire tunnel in just 4 days.
Saxicola rubicola: Stonechats get their name from their alarm call, which sounds exactly like two pebbles being clicked together.
Sciurus carolinensis: Grey squirrels can find buried nuts by smell even under a foot of snow. But they forget about 25% of their caches, accidentally planting thousands of trees.
Sciurus vulgaris: Red squirrels can hang upside down from branches and rotate their hind feet 180°. Their ankle joints are double-jointed for gripping in any direction.
Scolopax rusticola: Woodcock carry their chicks between their legs in flight to move them away from danger. One of the very few birds known to transport their young this way.
Sibirionetta formosa: Baikal teal breed in Siberia and are extremely rare vagrants to Britain. A sighting in the UK causes huge excitement among birdwatchers.
Sitta europaea: Nuthatches are the only British bird that can walk headfirst down a tree trunk. They don't use their tail for support like treecreepers and woodpeckers.
Spatula clypeata: Shovelers have comb-like structures on their bill edges that filter tiny crustaceans from water. They often feed in spinning circles to stir up food.
Spatula querquedula: Garganey are the only British duck that is a true summer migrant. They arrive from Africa in spring and leave again by autumn.
Spinus spinus: Siskins were once rare garden visitors. They only started regularly using bird feeders in the 1960s and are now one of the most common feeder birds.
Sterna hirundo: Common terns are nicknamed 'sea swallows' for their graceful forked tails. They plunge-dive from 10 metres up and can hover like a kestrel before striking.
Streptopelia decaocto: Collared doves only arrived in Britain in 1955. They spread across the entire country in just 20 years, one of the fastest natural colonisations ever recorded.
Strix aluco: Tawny owls are responsible for the classic 'twit-twoo' sound. But it's actually a duet: the female calls 'twit' (ke-wick) and the male answers 'twoo' (hoo-hoo-oo).
Sturnus vulgaris: Starling murmurations can contain over a million birds. They avoid collisions by each tracking just their seven nearest neighbours.
Sylvia atricapilla: Blackcaps have changed their migration in just 50 years. German birds now winter in Britain instead of Africa, evolving a completely new migration route.
Sylvia communis: Whitethroat populations crashed by 90% in a single year (1969) due to drought in their African wintering grounds. One of the first signs of climate affecting UK wildlife.
Tachybaptus ruficollis: Little grebes can barely walk on land. Their legs are set so far back on their body for swimming that they essentially fall over if they try.
Tadorna tadorna: Shelducks from all over northwest Europe gather at a single moulting site in the German Wadden Sea. Up to 100,000 birds become temporarily flightless together.
Talpa europaea: Moles can dig 20 metres of new tunnel per day and their saliva contains a toxin that paralyses earthworms. They store living but immobilised worms in underground larders.
Thalasseus sandvicensis: Sandwich terns got their name from Sandwich in Kent, where they were first scientifically described. Not from the food.
Tringa erythropus: Spotted redshanks are the only wader where the male does all the incubating and chick-rearing. The female leaves after laying and sometimes finds a second mate.
Tringa nebularia: Greenshanks have slightly upturned bills and wade belly-deep in water, sweeping their bill from side to side to catch small fish and shrimp.
Tringa totanus: Redshanks are nicknamed the 'sentinel of the marsh' because they're usually the first bird to spot danger and raise the alarm, warning every other species nearby.
Troglodytes troglodytes: The wren is one of Britain's tiniest birds but has one of the loudest songs. Their call is 10 times louder per gram of body weight than a cockerel's crow.
Turdus iliacus: Redwings navigate by the stars during migration. Experiments showed they become disoriented in planetariums when the star patterns are shifted.
Turdus merula: Blackbirds have adapted to city life so successfully that urban birds sing at higher frequencies than rural ones to be heard over traffic noise.
Turdus philomelos: Song thrushes smash snails on favourite 'anvil' stones. Individual birds return to the same stone repeatedly, leaving piles of broken shells around it.
Turdus pilaris: Fieldfares defend their nests by dive-bombing predators and deliberately defecating on them. The sticky droppings can matt a bird of prey's feathers enough to ground it.
Turdus viscivorus: Mistle thrushes are nicknamed 'stormcocks' because they sing loudly from exposed treetops in the worst winter gales, when every other bird has taken shelter.
Tyto alba: Barn owls fly almost silently thanks to comb-like fringes on their wing feathers that break up turbulence. Their hearing is so precise they can catch prey in total darkness.
Vanellus vanellus: Lapwings are named for their erratic, tumbling flight display. 'Lapwing' comes from Old English meaning 'leap with a wink', describing their aerial acrobatics.
Vulpes vulpes: Urban foxes in London have measurably shorter snouts and wider skulls than rural foxes. They're rapidly evolving to suit city life, similar to the process that produced domestic dogs.
